title: 2025 07 17 EDSI
displayTitle: 2025-07-17 - Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee - Review briefing on the 2023/24 annual review of the New Zealand Tourism Board
committee: Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee
topic: Review briefing on the 2023/24 annual review of the New Zealand Tourism Board
description: Thursday 17 July 2025 Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee Review briefing on the 2023/24 annual review of the New Zealand Tourism Board Hearing of evidence NZ Tourism Board Rene de Monchy, Chief Executive Paul Brock, Board Chair
descriptionSummary: Review briefing on the 2023/24 annual review of the New Zealand Tourism Board. NZ Tourism Board. Rene de Monchy, Chief Executive

chunks.text: Good morning. Morning, Morning. Morning. Now Just grab some water, if that's okay.
You are, yeah.
About something.

Morning. Thank you so much. Uh, we have one hour with you, so if you would like to start with, uh, in your introduction and some opening, uh, remarks, if you could keep them brief and then members, we will go on to questions. Thank you. Great. Well, thank you very much. So, I'm Renee De Manchi, the Chief Executive of Tourism New Zealand, Paul Brock, the Chair of Tourism New Zealand.

Thank you very much for your time. I'll make some brief opening remarks. So thank you for the opportunity to speak about our work and our performance over FY 24, the year in review. Um, happy to obviously answer any questions as we go. Um, it was certainly another important year, FY 24, uh, for the tourism sector.

International tourism being a critical contributor to the New Zealand economy. It has now recovered to the second largest export, uh, again, and for the year June, 2024 that we're reviewing international visitors contributed $11.6 billion to the economy, which was up 42% from 8.2 billion the year prior.

However, the remained there remained an opportunity for growth as total international visitor arrivals recovered to about 83%, uh, over that period of the review. New Zealand, uh, is a small player in the highly competitive international tourism market, and we're always gonna be a niche destination, so we need to continue to compete.

We have a really strong brand asset and a very flexible and familiar brand platform with a hundred percent pure New Zealand. That continues to be very successful after 25 years. Uh, in 2024 FY 24, the review period, we achieved 11 of our 15 performance targets. And from a brand perspective, we continue to see a hundred percent pure New Zealand and the, the, the platform of if you seek, perform strongly throughout that year.

Our marketing works for New Zealand as a holiday destination amongst the target audience at 46% preference, which was a seven year high. So good intent to travel. Uh, we supported during that period 97 conference bids within an estimated value of $148 million and 1 48 conferences worth $80 million for the year.

During that year. Uh, we also played hosted 54 international conferences, one through our business events conference assistance program that hosted 19,000 delegates and delivered a value of approximately $36 million during that year. So, in closing the future view as we look ahead, our ambition is certainly to ensure the industry continues to grow strongly with tourism playing a critical role in the government's ambition to double the value of exports over the next decade.

Uh, tourism must deliver high value economic outcomes for New Zealanders and must also maintain its social license alongside the economic growth. So it's encouraging to see during the period of this review, 94% of New Zealanders agree that tourism is good for New Zealand, and our current strategy is outlined in our 20 24, 20 28 statement of intent is really focused at growing year round to New Zealand, increasing the sector's value, um, to the economy.

In FY 25, the nominal visitor international spend surpassed 2019, however, arrivals plateaued at about 86% and we're getting on with the job of really growing those arrivals with recent boosts, um, to the tourism support program. And that's very much our primary goal for 2026.

So we're really excited about the opportunity ahead. There's plenty of potential for tourism to support the growth ambitions, uh, and the economy and our connectivity to the world. So with that, I'll leave it for questions. Thank you very much. Thank you. Um, we go to Glen Fu Uh, KDA. Thank you. And thanks for your time and for what you do for, um, for New Zealand and for our economy.

Um, and Brett telling us to the world. Uh, I'm just really keen to start pretty much where you finished around the, um, the statement of intent. Mm. And just can you just talk us through, just give us a bit of a, um, understanding of what it means for you as an organization. Obviously political priorities change. Mm-hmm. Um, they change, um, you've got a new set.

Um, what does that mean for you as an organization? Um, also, what are the challenges in terms of having to, I guess, I know words pivot or, you know, move from what was to what now is expected. Can you just talk to us a bit about those, um, those intents? Yeah. And, uh, fits for you as an organization? Yeah, sure. I'm happy to, um, to take that. We, so our statement of intent is our four year strategy, uh, for 20 24, 20 28.

It is fundamentally a growth strategy. How do we grow, uh, tourism, uh, and international tourism, obviously specifically, um, with a particular focus on how do we actually grow year round. There is a real challenge in the New Zealand tourism sector that, uh, uh, we have a strong summer and then the rest of the year is pretty tough going for a lot of tourism businesses.

So, particular focus of that, um, that continues to be our longer term strategy. But, uh, with the recent announcements from the government and our boost in funding and the tourism growth roadmap, there has been a kind of an accelerator put to that program. So how do we as part of that, get back the 2019 number of visitors in the short term, but in the medium to long term, very much still, how do we make New Zealand an appealing destination?

Year round is going to be really important for the productivity of the sector, but also for the ongoing investment in the sector, longer term jobs, all of those things. So they remain kind of, we're holding both of those things in terms of the short term lifting of, um, of the arrivals, but at the same time, uh, really promoting year round. And just in terms of as an organization, what does it mean for you as an organization financially, um, with your staff, with your teams having to sort of move from what was to now

what the next four year expectation is like, is there, is there a financial cost to that or what is the challenge for your team In terms of a new strategy setting? Yeah. Or, uh, we are in a, we are in the process now at the request of the Minister of Tourism Hospitality to actually reviewing our SOII see. Uh, as part of that. But that hasn't stopped the SPE for this financial year, Y 26.

So again, the fundamentals of our four year strategy. So no additional cost I don't think for, for reviewing that strategy. We do that in an annual basis. Fundamentally, the SOI was a growth strategy with a particular focus on growing the off peak. Um, we are reviewing that at the moment at the request of, uh, the minister of tourism and hospitality. So, so, yeah. Yeah.

So in terms of off peak, um, I understand that is a challenge. Yeah. Is it something you were already doing before, um, the government's expectations? And I mean, I was looking at the data and the, you know, the graphs you've got in your report that obviously shows that is a real challenge. Is it possible and what are the levers you need to pull to ensure that, that this can happen?

'cause it seems like quite a, a hard nut to crack or, or maybe rock. Yeah. Yeah. It, it certainly is. It is a tough challenge. And again, fundamentally we want to grow the tourism sector all year round. Mm-hmm. But there are different levers and different insights that gets people to try and come here in April and May than it maybe does in December and January.

Mm-hmm. So that's the first thing we started with going, what are the consumer insights of our audience? What are people looking for at different times of the year? Yeah. And that's been quite, um, enlightening. What we find for, you know, New Zealand is fundamentally appealing for its landscapes and scenery and the variety of activities that we can do at pretty close proximity.

Um, but what a lot of people don't realize is that a lot of those activities you can do every day of the year. You can do cycling tours of New Zealand most days of the year, you know, you can do many of those activities at VA famous. And so there's a part there about busting those myths, explaining that to consumers. Actually there are different times to travel. Um, but you're right, there's a little bit about understanding, you know, what are the motivators, um, to come at different times of the year.

But the prize is big because, um, if you're a hotelier, uh, you know, you have, you have good demand in those summer months, but it's pretty tough going, you know, now in July, for example, for for much of the country. So how do we get, and then, and then probably the last point I make is then there are specific, you know, season based activities that are attractive.

Skiing being an obvious one. Yeah. So how do we promote, come to New Zealand skiing? If you take China as an example, that's been a really emerging trend in that market. Um, much more interest in skiing and outdoor activities. So we've been over the last couple of years actually promoting skiing and outdoor winter activities much more aggressively in China as well. I think the other thing to add to that just is, um, if you look at which markets are having holidays at certain times of year, and therefore have a propensity to come to New Zealand,

those times of year, being targeting and being quite specific about going after those groups is a big, big part of what we do in that space as well. It's a long game though, that one. Yeah. Up. Yeah. Um, so I think you've been enrolled 10, 10 coming up 10 years. Uh, I've been CEO since, uh, April of 2021, but I've been with Tourism New Zealand For 10 years. Decade. Yeah.

Just in terms of, I guess, 'cause the off peak stuff is, I mean, I, I'm, I'm, I believe I agree. I think we need to, yeah. Is has there been any failings within Tourism New Zealand over, you know, the years that we've missed something? Like has there been a conversation over the years in your time and previously to get that thing? Because I, I look at, you know, we, we always sort of f Lord of the Rings and those sort of things that are Yeah.

Amazing experiences. We always seem to be winter focused, but then yeah, we've got those three months of some of it. Um, yeah, our peak time, I, I think we've, there's always been a degree of having a focus on, on, um, on growing year round, um, as well as a number of other things. Mm-hmm. Um, I dunno if there's been failures, but, uh, but it has been a really hard rock to move to your point.

Mm-hmm. So making a dent in that visitor profile is really hard. Now, obviously we had all the disruption of the COVID years. Yeah. But now coming out of COVID, actually making a dent in that visitor behavior will take ongoing efforts. I think ongoing making firstly consumers aware of what are the different things you can do at different times of the year.

You know, what's, and this is not about discouraging people from coming from the summer, not at all. But this is about giving other reasons. So, um, you know, other reasons to come to New Zealand that might be in the autumn, that might be to celebrate our food and wine. What is it? So it's really about, you know, feeding up those different itineraries, but it is a very difficult challenge to move. Yeah.

And my last one on this one, yeah. Okay. Is around, sorry, just around the statement of intent. Um, so it is around growth, it is around obviously trying to spread that out. It is around doubling, you know, tourism in 10 years. Yeah. But then sustainability is part of what you do as well. Yes. How do you reconcile the two? How do you do growth and sustain sustainability hand In hand? Yeah.

There's probably two, two parts to that. Um, one is going, we always try and, you know, pride ourselves on going, we want to listen to the voice of the visitors. So what are visitors looking for when they're in New Zealand? What we've seen in our research that we've done on, in, in, uh, over a number of years is an, is an increasing interest in sustainability credentials of the things that people are doing.

In fact, our audience is a higher propensity to be interested in environmental issues, sustainability than the general population, uh, from the studies that we've done. So one of the things we do through our subsidiary business, Qualmark, is we really encourage operators to clearly show their credentials of the activities that we are doing, uh, in, in sustainability in the broader sense of the word, in terms of engaging with their communities, um, but also environmental sustainability.

You know, we have a, um, international sustainability accreditation as part of your Qualmark membership. So that's a bit, I guess, from the bottom up. Um, the second is, um, you know, again, going back to year round, if you think about traveling, if we think about, um, all year round, there are, there is quite a lot of assets that are underutilized during that time of year.

So if you think about it, we have capacity that builds for our summer, but a lot of that capacity is underutilized through the year, rest of the year. So the most sustainable form of growth is also how do we, you know, build that growth into that year round. Uh, that's a strong partnership with industry to make sure that we understand those capacity issues and be careful and considered in the way that we're planning for that.

Mm-hmm. We can't predict everything, but it's obviously a key component of working on the supply side as well. Mm-hmm. Yep. So before we go to the next question, and remember this is through, uh, review briefing for 20 23, 20 24, but obviously, yeah. Uh, Scott Willis, Thank you. And I think possibly mine's a sub.

So I'm thinking about how do you balance your role to deliver economic returns with the urgent need to reduce emissions? Because most of, most of our tourism flies here, and that's, I know it's not your responsibility mm-hmm. But it is the pipeline that you influence. Mm-hmm. So what are you doing in that space?

Yeah. Uh, I'm happy to, to, to lead on that, Paul. Um, well, firstly, you're right. That is obviously a, a, a, a, a hot topic, um, for, and you're right where we are in the world, most visitors come here and have to fly here. Um, so there's a number of factors. One is Australia is our most significant inbound market in short haul market.

So if we look at current statistics, more than 40% of our leaders are actually Australians. Um, so that's the shortest haul market. Um, um, the second is we really encourage people, for, for a lot of people who come to New Zealand, it's the trip of a lifetime. So the length of stay that we, if you are going to fly here, burn the carbon to get here, staying as long as possible and undertaking as many activities as possible.

And that shows up in our average length of stay. Even in Australians, average length of stay is about nine days. Going up to a European, it's more like 24, 25 plus stays for a stay. So that's important. Mm-hmm. Um, and the third is, uh, goes back a little bit to the, to the product offering, is what we know from our research is people, visitors recognize that they have to fly here to get here, but how can they minimize their impact when they're here?

So again, um, operators giving, you know, really good sustainability offerings in terms of what they do, how they recycle, how they reduce their own emissions, that is gonna become increasingly important in visitors' decisions when they're here on the ground. Yeah. Yeah.

It's not, it's not really comparable a flight versus not, not throwing a towel out, but, you know, I get it. It's a good thing to do. Um, what's the waiting on the focus towards domestic tourism versus international tourism? Uh, we, we are a hundred percent internationally focused.

So you, what are you, what are you, are you, are you collaborating for our domestic tourists? Or you just don't care about the No, well, we, it's not in a mandate, it's not your mandate, but, but you know, But certainly domestic is a really important audience. Right. So if you look at the, to total domestic tourism sector, 60% of it is New Zealanders.

It's us getting out and about in our own country traveling. Mm. It's by far the biggest segment of tourism. And for many regions, it could be up to 75, 80% of visitors, uh, uh, will be domestic audiences. So hugely important. Um, we don't have a direct role, uh, in that we did during the COVID years when the borders were closed, we were, um, really active in promoting New Zealanders to get out and about in our own country.

Um, um, so we did play a role that, um, that ended, uh, when the borders opened, and we went back to being entirely internationally focused. But we work in close collaboration with, um, the regional tourism organizations. Uh, we are focused on international, but they are focused on domestic tourism. So we're aware of each other's work in that space, but we don't directly have a remit for domestic tourism.

Okay. And so you're talking about the going, the going for growth purpose of the government at the moment, then you are, you are aiming to grow tourism, but you've also got this a hundred percent pure brand. And, um, and that, that seems like there may well be a, a dilemma there. Mm. Because we've got more degraded water paint, we've got more, more degradation in our, in our natural systems.

We've got the potential for some unique landscapes to be dug into gold mines. Mm-hmm. How, how do, do you, do you have wider discussions about the impacts of other government policy that may well have a serious impact on, on the value of tourism? Yeah, we, we certainly monitor what potential visitors are thinking about New Zealand, how they view New Zealand, um, a hundred percent pure New Zealand.

And in fact, our most recent brand relaunch has really brought that to the fore, has always been about a hundred percent pure New Zealand experience. So the variety of things that you can undertake in New Zealand in the beautiful landscape and the beautiful scene absolutely outdoors is vastly important because that's the most important reason why people come here. We continue to monitor, um, visitor sentiment.

What we have seen, uh, over the year of this review period, and as at our latest data as well, is that New Zealand continues to perform really, really well as a destination for its beautiful landscapes and scenery, and a hundred percent pure helps to kind of bring that to the fore. Um, but absolutely, we, we, we monitor that.

If, if there are, um, if there are views of that changing amongst the consumers. We don't, we don't see it in our research at the moment. Alright. We Monitor perception. We, sorry, you were adding something Paul? Uh, there. No, we're just gonna say that we monitor perception and we don't see an issue with that at the moment. Alright. Uh, we go to Dr. Thank you Madam chair.

Um, so what do you think, uh, were the specific strategies that contributed to an a 42% increase in international visitor spending? What specific things had you done to achieve that? Yeah. Um, well it was, this was still one of the earlier recovery years coming out of COVID.

So the period of FI 24, um, was still, you know, the borders only opened in fully in August of 2022. So this was still very much a recovery year as part of that. But we certainly during the COVID years, had a real focus on how do we restart, um, the tourism economy, make sure that visitors are aware that we are, we are ready and we're open for business.

We partnered strongly with the industry. Mm-hmm. And particularly with the airline partners who were restarting their connectivity to New Zealand. Um, and then we had some really good tailwinds in FY uh, 24 as well. The FIFA Women's World Cup, uh, was during that par partially during that period as well. So that's, you know, that was to talk about seasonality.

That was a fantastic boost in the arm for July August of 2023. Um, you know, having the American base team here, we had more than 20,000 Americans come during that period. Um, so they were, you know, that was a strong tailwind that certainly helped that recovery. Mm-hmm. And, um, just on sub on that, were there any specific media partnerships during that time that, um, contributed to that?

Um, $307, uh, $307 million in advertising. Yes. We did a lot of partnerships actually. So we work across, uh, multiple platforms. Some of it is in paid advertising, which we would do in actually in most media formats in, in most of the world. Uh, we are particularly, uh, digital marketing.

So we don't do a lot of broadcast marketing, with the exception of Australia because we're quite a big share of Australian travel. Whereas in, in some of the long markets, like the US for example, we're about a quarter of a percent of their outbound travel. So we are, so we are more in the digital marketing space. But to your point earlier around, um, some of the advertising value created, we do work a lot with user generated content.

So influences travel writers, you know, people who come here and experience New Zealand share that experience. Uh, and how we rate that is the advertising value. If we had paid for that space, what would that have cost us? Uh, and we had a really strong year in FY 24, you know, partly 'cause we're still in that recovery mode. So people had a really, you know, renewed interest in New Zealand and exploring New Zealand. Good.

Thank you. Good. Um, I have a sub, uh, to that question. So with the, the announcement that from November, um, people, those who hold Chinese passport will be able to come from Australia to New Zealand without requiring another visa if they have a valid visa for Australia for up to three months. So you said that the numbers comparing 2019 to last year, I mean they were increasing plateau at 85%.

Mm-hmm. So what kind of, uh, impact do you see with that policy coming into effect from November? Yeah. Have you kind of assessed what it would look like? Yeah, I, I think it will certainly help. Um, so to give the context, we currently plateau at about 86% total arrivals, but within that, China is only at about 60% recovered.

Mm-hmm. Now, there's a variety of reasons for that. Part of that is a bit the traveler mix. So in historical numbers, there were quite a lot of shorter stay group travel, but they're unlikely to be the visitors that come back. That was a, you know, those price points that they were achieving are not, are not likely to be hit.

Um, but certainly visas, you know, travel is about how do you get motivators, uh, um, so what we track in our research is what are the motivations for coming, and then what are the barriers for coming? And things like visas, flight times cost, all of those things are barriers and are friction points. So removing, um, so the, the, the government's announcement that from November, uh, a visitor will be able to go, if they're in Australia, they'll be able to come on to New Zealand on that visa will just remove that friction point.

And anywhere up to about a third of Chinese visitors will be what we call dual destination. They go to Australia and New Zealand. So I think that will absolutely help, um, to remove that friction point. Mm-hmm. Um, Sub from Glen Bennett. Yeah. Thank you. Just in terms of, um, value for money, um, so in terms of, did you say it's the amount of money you spend, is that you just, you figured that out from there?

For example, you use the Australian breakfast tv Yes. Indian influences. Um, you talked about the World Cup, obviously the Women's World Cup. Um, like how do you, how are you actually able to properly measure that? Yeah. In terms of, you know, breakfast TV in Australia? Yeah. Well, what we do is we use, we work with our PR agency and you can, you can have a, there's a, there's a model that says if I was to, you know, so I get exposure, let's say it's number of minutes on air, or it's, um, in traditional terms space in a newspaper,

or it might be social sentiment, all of that, there's a value to that. I could buy that as an advertiser and then I, it would cost me a certain amount. And so that, that's what they do. They make those two comparisons. You know, breakfast, TV you get from Australia, you get five mornings, you get, you know, half hour cross live to Australia, uh, to back into Australian households.

You know, if I was to try and buy those slots on TV for that period of time, there's a value attached to that. So it's not a perfect measure, but it's a pretty good proxy measure in terms of, it costs me x amount of dollars to get that TV show here. The broadcast value of that is y you know, what's the return on investment? And, um, it's a pretty well used model, um, in terms of measuring impact, value, impact of, uh, PR activities. Mm-hmm.

Uh, new primary from Scott Lewis. Um, what are you, what are you hearing from those in the tourism and tourism businesses about, uh, going for growth or growing the sector? Because certainly what we are hearing on the ground is that people have more, seeking more of a steady state sustainable practice rather than any expansion, any growth.

And they're seeking that for a quality of life. Mm-hmm. And, and, and lifestyle. So what are you hearing on the ground? Are you hearing something different, you're hearing people want to grow and, and do, you know, own multimillion dollar houses and you know, and do become wealthy and, you know, or what are, or you, or are you hearing what we are hearing?

We here, that's exactly what we're hearing. Me. You go, yeah. Look, I certainly, we, we spend most of our time talking to tourism operators and then obviously monitoring sentiment as well. Uh, tourism operators, there is definitely an enthusiasm, enthusiasm for growth. So whether that's airline partners, whether that's hoteliers that have invested in those properties, um, that see, you know, if you take Auckland hoteliers for example, they're having a pretty tough winter this year.

They've got big infrastructure that they've built on. So they're definitely behind the aspiration, uh, for growth. If you take it at a simple level, we are not back to the number of visitors we had back in 2019. So we have capacity certainly to grow. And then it's about certainly how do we grow? Well, we want visitors to have a great experience.

We want 'em to go travel widely around the country. We want 'em to come at different times of the year. So that grows the productivity of the sector. But overwhelmingly, the sentiment I hear from tourism operators is a real support and desire to grow the sector. So, yeah. I'm, I'm surprised we are clearly talking to two different parts sector.

I mean, clearly overwhelmingly we are hearing the exact opposite. So that's possibly because I'm green and we go, yeah. We go to green focused tourism operators. Yeah. Um, how, in that case, how widely are you? Are you looking, you know, how widely are you talking in the sector?

Well, we work with a, with a, I mean, there are, you know, up to about 10,000 tourism operators in New Zealand if you want. I can't, we can't talk to all of those, but we certainly engage widely with them. Uh, we share our annual plans with them. We attend a lot of tourism conferences around the country. Uh, we do a lot of partnership work with tourism operators and share that with them.

Uh, we survey tourism operators to get sentiment. Uh, we track the mood of the nation study that we do in collaboration with the tourism industry arts to get public sentiment and New Zealanders sentiment of tourism. So I guess a variety of those, those touch points. Alright. Uh, supplementary from, uh, Glen Bennett. Yeah, thank you. In your, in Your report, you do have a su um, sustainability strategy.

Um, and part of that is around emission emissions reduction, but it looks like in there it is more at the moment you're just doing an assessment of your emissions rather than actually Yeah. Reducing 'em. Is that correct? So that is in FY 23, uh, uh, sorry, FY 24 as per the government's direction in terms of us as an agency also reducing our emissions. Yeah. So we are actively tracking that to today.

So this was the foundation year, uh, and then there are targets, uh, aligned to the government's targets of its own, uh, uh, emission reductions. And we are tracking and on track, uh, for that as tourism New Zealand, the agency. Alright. Uh, supplementary from Sam Offender. Thank you. Thank you very much, Madam Chair to meet You.

Very refreshing to hear a member of the green party talking about the environment, but I know just on on that the, the line of discussion there around, um, tourism operators and growth, or not growth, I mean, um, we've heard from that member around sort of a, a, a degrowth model and you know, on the lines of sustainability, steady state.

Alright. When you, when you talk to, um, tourism operators, you know, the hotel motel owners, people who employ lots of people, everyone who is involved in tourism industry, um, the, the cafes and everyone associated with that, do they want more international tourists in New Zealand or do they want us to degrowth international tourism?

They want more international tourists in New Zealand. Okay. Why is that? That's the well, because that is what their core business is, uh, yeah. Is um, is delivering to those visitors. Uh, you know, and, and, and particularly if so tac to hospitality sector, cafes and restaurants is a good example.

If you're an international visitor, you're likely to go out for a meal at least once, if not twice a day. So international visitors are a really important part to being a cafe owner in Kaiko or in Rota or in Queenstown or, or even in Auckland. You know, Tara, Tara, another great example. Thank you.

And I think also they spend more Yes. You know, when on average per visitor than what we're spending locally as well. So, you know, that's a driver from our perspective for those businesses. So international tourists are good for New Zealand. Yeah. Good. A supplementary from, uh, Dr. David Wilson. Um, first of all, compliment the team over there at sliding from seasonality to various other issues after the primary question.

But anyway, um, I'll go back to that one. Seasonality. Are you a supplementary or a primary? Uh, you indicated a supplementary, So the primary was seasonality. So supplementary to that. Supplementary, yeah. Yep. So that's part of your SOI looking forward still. Yes. And, um, having been involved in, you know, running an RTO and so on, I know how hard it is to turn the dial on that and some region regions have managed queenstown, others haven't Northland mm-hmm.

Um, very high seasonality. Yep. So I just want to ask you a question. While you are reconsidering your SOI, how long do we kick this horse? Because shoulder season is, seems to me is a slightly better avenue for us to increase. And I know there are capacity constraints.

I know that the industry itself has capacity constraints. So can you just give us some insight into that? What's your thinking moving forward? Yeah, yeah. No, great question. Thank, thank the member for the question. I think, look, it is a really stubborn problem. A as you will well know from Northland. In fact, Northland is much more seasonal than many other parts of the country.

Your, your peak season's probably about six weeks from kind of Christmas day until Ang weekend. So it's an even narrower kind of period of time. But you're quite right. When we talk about year round and when we talk about off peak for simplicity reasons, we've said off peak is March to November. But you're quite right within that.

There's a, there's a lot of, there's a lot of scope. So the first, and, and, and what you've seen in the first year of the strategy has been, for example, getting encouraging Americans to also consider New Zealand in March and April. Not necessarily coming in in July and August, but kind of going look, have you considered just an extension of the summer, the shoulder seasons to use, to use your words?

I, I think that is gonna be crucially important. Um, growing the tourism sector year round, I think is going to be crucial, important for the long-term sustainable growth of the tourism sector and the aspiration to double the value of exports. We must continue down that track because, and, and, and it's simply that there are also different tools to sell different times of the year.

Yeah. So there's capacity in the summer, but if we want people to come here at different times of the year, there might be slightly different activities, different itineraries, different types of marketing that we do to encourage people, uh, at that time of year. So myself on that one is, um, we've been pushing this bar for over a decade. Mm-hmm. Have we moved the dial at all? Not Stanley books.

We've seen, we've seen green shoots, we've seen green shoots. Uh, but you're right, it is, you have to keep working at it.
